docs.title: Home
docs.text: CardPG is a roleplaying game that sees players exploring dungeons, pushing forward through multiple objectives until their stamina runs out, and they need to return to the safety of town.

docs.title: Primary Rolling Mechanic
docs.text: CardPG uses Fudge Dice. Fudge Dice are D6, and each die has two blank sides, two sides with a +, and two sides with a -. To complete any test, you roll 4DF, and the pluses and minuses cancel out. This leaves you with a numerical result between -4 and +4, with middle results far more likely. This will be added to one of the Skills, set by the GM.

docs.title: Characters
docs.text:
Your Character will be created by making a series of choices.

Ancestry: Beastkin, Construct, Dwarf, Fae, Goblin, Halfling, Human, Outsider

Background: Criminal, Exile, Farmer, Labourer, Merchant, Military, Monastic, Noble, Scholar, Wilds

Class: Barbarian, Bard, Cleric, Druid, Knight, Mage, Mercenary, Monk, Psychic, Ranger, Rogue, Soldier, Sorcerer, Summoner, Swashbuckler, Witch

Weapon: Bow, Brawling, Crossbow, Cudgel, Daggers, Greataxe, Greatsword, Holy Symbol, Mageblade, Rapier, Staff, Sword & Shield, Tome, Wand, Warhammer, Whipblade

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: • Build Tester — pick an ancestry, background, class, subclass, weapon, and level, then browse the resulting card pool and try building a hand. Loads live from this site's own data, so it's always current. Opens in a new tab (it's a standalone tool, not a guide page).

docs.title: Beastkin
docs.text:
Humanoids with some animal features. Beastkin can be found throughout Veleris Coast, but tend to stay in small communities with their kin. In combat, Beastkin are fierce and mobile fighters.

If you choose to be a Beastkin, describe what animal you resemble.

Starting Health: 6

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Pounce 1 20 Move 3 spaces, then attack 2. Feral Rush 1 25 Move 2 spaces, then attack 2 and apply Bleed 2 to the target. Savage Leap 5 18 Expend: Move 4 spaces, then attack 3 and apply Bleed 2 to adjacent targets. Pack Instinct 5 22 Move 3 spaces, then attack 2. Allies within 2 spaces move 2.

docs.title: Construct
docs.text:
Constructs are creatures created with cobbled together parts and magic that binds a soul to the new body. Some constructs are made of dead tissue, while others are crafted of wood, metal, and stone. Constructs are rare, but prized as tireless workers. In combat, Constructs empower themselves for extra damage.

If you choose a construct, determine what you were made of, and whose soul now animates you.

Starting Health: 7

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Recalibrate 1 38 Gain Boost, then move 2 spaces. Gear Lock 1 45 Apply Slow to one enemy, then attack 2. Overclock 5 30 Ongoing: gain haste and boost at the start of the next 3 rounds. Mechanical Arms 5 40 Attack 3, then push the target 2 spaces.

docs.title: Dwarf
docs.text:
In the Veleris Coast, Dwarves have long abandoned their underground cities in the mountains, and have become a common sight in most settlements. Dwarves are hardy and traditional. In combat, Dwarves excel when taking defensive position against incoming attacks.

If you choose a Dwarf, determine where you hail from and what you carry to tie you to your ancestral home.

Starting Health: 7

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Iron Grudge 1 55 Attack 2, then gain Retaliate 2. Bulwark 1 68 Gain Bolster 2, then attack 2. Sunder 5 50 Attack 3 with Pierce 2, then gain Bolster 2. Mountain Fortress 5 72 Gain Bolster 3 and Retaliate 3.

docs.title: Fae
docs.text:
Most Fae in Veleris Coast can be found in the great Fey city of Leriadar, but there are no shortage of Fey creatures who have found their way to the other parts of the land, seeking adventure. Innately magical, Fae have deep ties to the land. In combat, Fae use magic to avoid damage and inflict conditions.

If you choose a Fae, decide what Fey creature you are, and what pushed you into a life of adventure.

Starting Health: 5

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Faerie Flame 1 28 Attack 2 and apply Burn to the target, then move 1 space. Will-o-Wisp 1 35 Move 2 spaces, then apply Blind to one enemy. Faerie Inferno 5 32 Attack 2 against all enemies in a 3-space burst and apply Burn to each. Glamour 5 40 Apply Blind to all enemies within three spaces, then move 2 spaces.

docs.title: Goblin
docs.text:
Goblins are eager, clever, and endlessly enthusiastic. While they still face some prejudice, especially from Dwarves and Fae, they are excellent hunters, and run many of the most efficient caravan operations in the land. Most towns in the area now feature goblin warrens. In combat, Goblins are remarkably mobile, darting around the battlefield.

If you choose a Goblin, determine where your warren is, and why you are no longer welcome there.

Starting Health: 5

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Scatter 1 15 Move 3 spaces, then attack 1. Sneak In 1 20 Move 2 spaces and gain Hide. Blitz 5 18 Move 3 spaces, then attack 2. Vanish Strike 5 22 Move 3 spaces, attack 2, then gain Hide.

docs.title: Halfling
docs.text:
Stealthy and cautious, Halflings are welcome everywhere, running reliable farms and cozy shops. Both of the Halfling towns have fully sunk into the swamps, making most Halflings recent refugees. Large caravans of Halflings have traveled North and West through the mountains, but many have also stayed closer to home. In combat, Halflings are stealthy, but have surprising attacks.

If you choose a Halfling, explain which community you hail from, and describe why you didn't travel with that community beyond the mountains.

Starting Health: 5

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Action Fade 1 12 Move 2 spaces and gain Hide. Pocket Knife 1 20 Attack 2 and apply Bleed 2 to the target, then gain Hide. Shadow Slip 5 15 Ongoing: At the end of your next three turns, gain Hide. Cheap Shot 5 18 Attack 3 with Pierce 1 and apply Bleed 2 to the target.

docs.title: Human
docs.text:
Humans live throughout the realm, as varied as the communities that they create. Those who have stayed through the recent struggles tend to be highly resilient and stubborn, or else don't have any other option. In combat, humans are mobile and flexible.

If you choose to be a human, explain which settlement you grew up in, and what brought you to Brewold.

Starting Health: 6

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Surge 1 25 Expend: Attack 4. Recover 1 random exiled card. Momentum 1 30 Attack 2, then move 2 spaces. Persistence 5 22 Move 2 and Attack 1. Then repeat. Reposition 5 28 Attack 3, then move 2 spaces.

docs.title: Outsider
docs.text:
Outsiders have found themselves on this plane of existence. They may be a bastard child of a divine or infernal being, or some other otherworldly creature trapped on the mundane plane. They are rare, but generally left alone by those who fear them. In combat, Outsiders use their powers to debilitate their foes.

If you are an outsider, describe the features that expose your otherworldly heritage.

Starting Health: 6

docs.title: Cards
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Void Touch 1 42 Attack 1 and apply Stun to the target, then apply Curse 2 to them. Unravel 1 48 Ongoing:Now and the start of your next two turns, adjacent enemies gain weaken 2 and Blind. Entropy 5 45 Attack 2 and apply Curse 2 to the target, then apply Slow to them. Teleport 5 52 Move 6.

docs.title: Criminal
docs.text: You grew up as a criminal or spent some time imprisoned. Now, you either want to escape that past or return to it.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Cover Story 1 28 Gain Hide, then restore 1 card from your discard (your choice).

docs.title: Exile
docs.text: You grew up far away from home, either in hiding or cast out from your society. For you, the adventuring party represents the closest thing to family that you've ever had.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Forced March 1 32 Move 3 spaces, then restore 1 card from your discard (your choice).

docs.title: Farmer
docs.text: You grew up on a farm, now swallowed by swampy marshland. You are used to hard work, but also better times.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Harvest Swing 1 42 Attack 2, then heal 2 HP on yourself.

docs.title: Labourer
docs.text: You grew up doing hard labour. It was a monotonous life, but left you with useful skills and a strong back. At times, a dangerous adventure feels almost like a vacation.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Endure 1 65 Gain Bolster 2, then heal 2 HP on yourself.

docs.title: Merchant
docs.text: You grew up in the merchant class, helping to run a shop or running shipments from town to town. You have an eye for value, and also understand economics better than your fellow travelers.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Risky Deal 1 48 Recover 2 cards from your exile, then discard 2 cards.

docs.title: Military
docs.text: You grew up in military service, but are no longer bound to service. You see your new party as a new military unit, even though most of them lack discipline.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Iron Will 1 60 Ongoing: Gain Retaliate 3. At the start of each round, gain Bolster (ends when you no longer have retaliate).

docs.title: Monastic
docs.text: You grew up as part of a monastic order, whether in a scholarly role or through isolation and training. Either way, you have been sent out to explore the world, for some important purpose.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Inner Peace 1 62 Heal 3 HP on yourself, then clear 1 condition from yourself.

docs.title: Noble
docs.text: You come from privilege, and grew up with wealth and power. You have left that life behind, either through rebellion or necessity. This life is very different, but you are starting to adjust.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Noble Command 1 58 Apply Slow to one enemy, then you and an ally restore one card from discard.

docs.title: Scholar
docs.text: You grew up learning about the world, but experiencing little of it. Now, you are right in the middle of it, discovering quickly that book lessons can only get you so far.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Field Notes 1 55 Restore 2 cards from your discard (your choice).

docs.title: Wilds
docs.text: You grew up as an outlander, either a nomadic existence or a community living close to nature. For you, even small towns feel impossibly busy, but still get lots of opportunities to travel through the wild spaces.

docs.title: Recovery Card
docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Nature's Call 1 35 Recover 1 random card from your exile, then move 2 spaces.

docs.title: Barbarian
docs.text:
The barbarian is a front-line fighter, who uses their card pool as an additional resource. Many of the barbarian's cards require discards for additional effects, but will need to rest more often.

Passive: When you take damage, your next attack this round deals 1 additional damage.

Starting HP: 8

Recommended Weapons: Greataxe, Greatsword, Cudgel, Warhammer, Brawling

docs.text: Card Level Initiative Ability Rush In 1 6 Move 5 Shoulder Charge 1 10 Discard 1 card: Move 3 spaces, then Attack 2. Improvise 1 24 Restore 1 card from your discard pile, then Discard 2 cards. Throwing Axe 1 34 Attack 2, with Bleed 1 at a target within 5 spaces. Enraged 1 56 Ongoing: At the start of your turn, discard a card and gain Boost and Bolster 2. End this effect if you cannot discard any cards. Second Wind 1 75 Discard a card. Heal 3 and Recover 1 from your exile. Boulder Toss 1 85 Discard a card: Attack a target within 3 spaces for 2, pushing them back 1. Regeneration 2 43 Ongoing: Heal 1 every time you discard a card. End this effect if you reach max health or have no cards in your discard pile. Hurl 2 65 Attack 2, then push target 3 spaces. If the target collides with another target, Attack each target 2. Beatdown 7 19 Attack 2, then Move 3, then Attack 2. Slam 7 32 Attack X, where X is the number of cards in your discard pile. Warlust 10 10 Expend: Discard your remaining hand. For each card discarded this way, attack 2 against one enemy of your choice. You may choose different targets for each attack. Death Lunge 10 15 Expend: Move 5 spaces, then attack 5 with Pierce 3 and Bleed 3. If this attack brings the target to 0 HP, restore your entire discard pile to your hand.

docs.title: Bard
docs.text:
The Bard is a support caster, providing buffs and debuffs in wide areas, subtly affecting the flow of each battle.

Passive: Whenever you target another ally with an effect, they Cure 1 condition.

Starting HP: 5

Recommended Weapons: Crossbow, Holy Symbol, Staff, Tome, Wand

docs.title: Cleric
docs.text:
The Cleric provides healing and buffs to allies, while also having the ability to stand in the midst of the battle when needed.

Passive: Whenever you target an ally with a buff, heal them 1.

Starting HP: 6

Recommended Weapons: Cudgel, Holy Symbol, Staff, Tome, Warhammer

docs.title: Druid
docs.text:
The Druid uses the power of nature to summon Totems to support zones of allies, and uses nature magic to participate in the fight.

Passive: Your summons have +1 armor.

Starting HP: 6

Recommended Weapons: Bow, Cudgel, Mageblade, Staff, Wand, Whipblade

docs.title: Psychic
docs.text:
The Psychic uses mental attacks that bypass enemy defenses, and mind control abilities to coerce enemies to attack each other. Requires thoughtful use of abilities for maximum effect.

Passive: When you kill an enemy, regain 2 health.

Starting HP: 5

Recommended Weapons: Bow, Crossbow, Holy Symbol, Tome, Wand, Whipblade

docs.title: Ranger
docs.text:
The Ranger attacks from a distance, and can leave traps in spaces to cause damage and inflict conditions onto anyone stupid enough to walk into (or get pushed into) those spaces. Keep enemies at a distance and pepper them with damage.

Passive: Gain 1 additional range on ranged attacks.

Starting HP: 5

Recommended Weapons: Bow, Crossbow, Daggers, Wand, Whipblade

docs.title: Sorcerer
docs.text:
The Sorcerer has the best spells for damage, but few defensive options and many cards with Expend. Make careful choices and use your resources when it counts. The rest of the time, toss around bolts of energy with impunity.

Passive: You may make basic attacks at Range 5.

Recommended Weapons: Crossbow, Staff, Sword & Shield, Tome, Wand, Whipblade

docs.title: Adventuring
docs.text:
As you travel to and explore dungeons, you will face a number of challenges, ranging from traps to social encounters to traversal puzzles and hidden pathways.

When you attempt a challenging action, the GM will give you a difficulty, ranging from trivial (0) to impossible (7). You will roll 4DF and add the relevant skill.

Roll Results

  • Below the target: failure
  • Meet the target: Succeed with drawback
  • Exceed the target: Succeed
  • Succeed with at least 3 over target: Succeed with benefit

You will also have a pool of Fate Points. you begin each session with 3 fate points. Players earn fate points by:

  • Offering a compel (using an aspect as a disadvantage)
  • Accepting a compel (accepting when the GM suggests using an aspect as a disadvantage)
  • Recovering a point through the combat Luck Die.

Players can spend Fate Points to:

  • Refuse a compel from the GM
  • Reroll the dice
  • Invoke an aspect (use an aspect to get +2 on a roll)

docs.title: Aspects
docs.text:
Aspects are used during Adventuring.

These include your Identity, Baggage, Drive, and Role

They should be written as a double-edged sword, something that can sometimes help you and sometimes hinder you during adventure. For instance, if you are a Goblin Swashbuckler, you have the Identity:

shaped by the underworld and the deals struck there

When one of your aspects would be helpful in the situation, you can invoke one of your aspects by spending one of your Fate Points. You get +2 on your roll.

If our swashbuckler is trying to find a place to sell some illicit goods, he could spend a fate point to get a +2 to his Underworld roll to find the right part of town.

However, your aspects are not always beneficial. If you are trying to use Personable to make a good impression at court, you or the GM might suggest taking a Compel, rolling at -2 but gaining a Fate Point for your trouble.

docs.title: Card Pool
docs.text:
Your pool of cards will be determined by your character creation choices.

You will use your card pool to determine a hand of 12 cards for your adventure.

Level Cards unlocked Source Notes 1 5 class Core class identity — players choose 4 of 5 for their pool 1 7 weapon Core weapon identity — players choose 5 of 7 for their pool 1 2 ancestry Abilities granted by your ancestry 1 1 background A single card that focuses on recovery 2 2 class Added class cards for further customization 3 2 weapon Advanced weapon moves - get both 4 1 subclass Subclass chosen here: hand size increases to 13 5 2 ancestry Upgrades to the level 1 ancestry cards 6 2 weapon Further weapon specialisation 6 1 subclass Second subclass card: hand size increases to 14 7 2 class Strong class upgrade cards 8 1 subclass Final subclass card; completes the set. Hand size: 15 9 2 weapon "Ultimate" — new core moves, always in demand 10 2 class "Ultimate" — significant power, most likely to be expended

docs.title: Character
docs.text: Your character is defined by the choices that you make during character creation:

docs.title: Ancestry
docs.text: Your character will be a Human, Fae, Dwarf, Goblin, Outsider, Construct, Beastkin, or Halfling. Your ancestry will give you 2 cards in combat, and contribute to your starting HP.

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
You will also choose how your character grew up. This will give you one card in combat, and will determine your main form of recovery.

  • Noble
  • Farmer

  • Military

docs.title: Baggage, Roles and Drives.
docs.text:
Your Aspects are determined by the combination of choices you've made already.

Your Ancestry and Background determine your Baggage, the thing that pushed you into your life of adventure.

Your Ancestry and Class determine your Role, the relationship that you have to your party.

Your Background and Class determine your Drive, a personal goal that motivates you.

docs.title: Combat
docs.text: Once combat begins, CardPG becomes a very different game. When combat begins, the group will be given a clear objective to meet for combat to end. Combat will end when the objective has been met, or all enemies are defeated.

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text: Characters carry Equipment that can aid them in combat. Most equipment can be purchased between adventures, but some can also be found as loot.

docs.title: Mechanics
docs.text:
You will have a hand of cards selected from your Card Pool, and each round you will select two of those cards to use that turn. You will choose one of your selected cards to establish your place in the initiative order. Initiative is ascending, from 1-99. On your turn, you can use both cards, in one of three ways:

  • Use the ability on the card
  • Make a basic attack (2 damage)
  • Make a basic move (3 spaces)

Whenever you make an attack, you will roll 4DF and the Luck Die. You will add or subtract your die result from the base value of the attack, which will determine the damage dealt.

Once a card has been used, it goes to your discard pile.

docs.title: Armor and Damage
docs.text:
Both characters and enemies have an armor value. When an attack deals damage, armor reduces it by its full value — 5 damage against a target with 2 armor deals 3 damage. If damage is reduced to zero or below, no damage is dealt. Status effects will be triggered, even if an attack value is 0.

The Pierce keyword is spent against Bolster first, and any leftover reduces effective armor. Bypass ignores the Shield buff entirely but does not affect armor.

Bolster: for the next several attacks against you — one per remaining stack — reduce damage by 1 each. Each time you take a hit, one Bolster stack is consumed (whether or not the attack deals damage). Bolster stacks do not expire at end of round — they last until used up.

Shield negates entire attacks. Each incoming attack consumes one Shield stack and deals 0 damage. The Bypass keyword ignores Shield entirely.

docs.title: Luck Dice
docs.text:
Players and enemies both have a D20 luck die, rolled along with the 4DF.

Player Die

Roll Effect 1 Card is expended (moves to exile instead of discard) 2 Miss — attack fails 3–17 No effect 18 Gain Fate Point 19 Card returns to hand (not discarded) 20 Critical — double dealt damage

Enemy Die

Roll Effect 1 Miss — attack fails 2 Enemy is Slowed 3–17 No effect 18 Enemy inflicts Exhaust (target discards a card) 19 Enemy inflicts Muddle (target exiles a card) 20 Critical — double dealt damage

If the roller is Poisoned, rolls 18–20 have no effect — Poison suppresses positive Luck Die outcomes for both players and enemies.

docs.title: Rounds and Turns
docs.text:
At the start of each round, players will select their cards in secret, and enemies will choose an action for the turn. One of these cards will be your lead card, and its initiative value will determine the initiative order (ascending).

If you have fewer than two cards in hand, you must rest instead.

  • Short Rest: One of your discarded cards is exiled at random, and you recover the rest. You can play your turn as normal.
  • Long Rest: You recover all discarded cards from your discard pile. Your initiative is set to 99 this turn, and you will not act this round.

If you are Poisoned, resting only recovers half of the eligible cards (rounded up) — you choose which.

On your turn, you can use both cards you selected.

Once all combatants have acted, end-of-round keywords will trigger, all timed status effects will tick down, and a new round will begin.

docs.title: Ongoing Cards
docs.text:
Some cards have the Ongoing keyword. When you play one of these, instead of going to your discard pile it moves to your ongoing zone, where it remains active until the effect ends.

Ongoing cards may apply effects at the start or end of each round, react to events like being attacked, or summon creatures that act on your behalf.

You can choose to end one of your own ongoing cards whenever you want, so long as it's not during another player or enemy's action. Send it to your discard pile as normal. An enemy's Stun can also end one of your ongoing cards.

docs.title: Exhaustion and Death
docs.text:
Each player and enemy has a health value. Attacks will deal damage to health. Any time you take damage, you may exile a card from your hand or discard pile instead of taking damage. This represents your character becoming tired, and losing access to your full suite of options.

If your health would be reduced to 0 and you cannot exile a card from your hand or discard pile, your character is killed.

docs.title: Fleeing
docs.text:
Running out of cards is dangerous, but it isn't the same as dying. If, at the start of a round, your hand and discard pile combined have fewer than two cards — nothing to select from, and nothing to rest with — your character flees the fight.

  • You survive to fight another day.
  • You are locked out of future gold and XP rewards until you return to Camp and Town. (Gold and XP you've already earned this adventure are not lost.)

Fleeing and dying are the two ways an adventure can end badly for a character — dying is permanent, fleeing is not.

docs.title: Field Rest
docs.text:
Between fights, you get a short time to catch your breath. This happens automatically as soon as the encounter ends:

  • All of your discarded, active, and ongoing cards return to hand.
  • Your equipment resets
  • You lose all buffs and conditions
  • 2 cards are restored from your exile zone straight back into your hand (all of them, if you have fewer than 2 exiled).
  • You heal 5 health.

docs.title: Dungeons
docs.text:
A dungeon is an area for adventuring, that the group will need to explore without any opportunities for safe rest. Each dungeon will have a series of objectives connected to it.

Some of these objectives will be known (likely tied to a nearby settlement), and others will be hidden. Discovering and resolving these objectives will earn the group XP.

docs.title: Minor Enemies
docs.text: Most enemies you encounter will be minor enemies. Fights with normal enemies will not normally threaten a party, but they will cost you cards, so fights with normal enemies can leave you exhausted over time.

docs.title: Normal Enemies
docs.text: Normal enemies typically have between 5-10 HP. When you kill a minor enemy, each party member gets 1 gold.

docs.title: Elite Enemies
docs.text: Elite enemies will be marked with a gold star. Elite enemies drop 2 gold on death for each party member, and differ from regular enemies: * +3 health * +1 damage on all attacks

docs.title: Champion Enemies
docs.text: Champion enemies offer a more significant threat. They are marked with a gold crown. * +6 health * +1 damage on all attacks * Take 2 actions per round

docs.title: Major Enemies
docs.text: The game also features major enemies, or bosses. Most dungeons will contain 2-3 bosses. Unlike minor enemies, bosses do not drop gold, but defeating a boss will usually trigger XP gain. As well, each boss will drop a Trophy, a piece of equipment that can only be used once. Trophies are powerful enough to shift the tide of a fight.

docs.title: Regular Bosses
docs.text: Regular bosses are designed to present a genuine threat to players, especially if they are supported by some minor enemies. Many bosses will have multiple phases, with new moves for each phase. Like players, bosses select 2 actions per turn, and will always act on the faster of the two actions.

docs.title: Bounty Bosses
docs.text: Most dungeons will also feature at least one Bounty Boss, which are normally not part of the main story of the dungeon. These bosses will be marked with a red skull. These are the most powerful enemies in the game, and will be a challenge for even a fully rested party. Bounty Bosses will not attack players on sight, so it will always be up to the party whether to engage with these enemies. Bounty Bosses often guard troves of treasure.

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text:
You have five equipment slots, that work much like cards.

Armor: Your armor may grant you passive armor, as well as having active abilities that prevent damage.

Headgear: Helmets can protect you from critical hits, but can also have a number of other practical uses.

Trinket: Trinkets have minor beneficial effects that can get you out of a tight spot

Potions: Potions are used for restoration, healing or restoring cards to keep you in the fight.

Trophy: Major enemies drop Trophies, powerful single-use items that can turn the tide of a fight.

docs.title: Fate Points
docs.text:
Fate points are a meta currency that allow you to get benefits on your rolls through your Aspects.

Every time you set out on an adventure, you start with 3 Fate Points.

docs.title: Game Flow
docs.text:
The overall flow of the game is as follows:

The group will be operating out of a settlement. NPCs at that settlement will offer tasks that can be taken on by the group. Every settlement has a few Dungeons nearby, which offer adventuring opportunities. At the settlement, the party will use Adventuring skills to learn more information.

Then, the group will set out to a Dungeon. Before they set out, they need to:

  • Determine a hand of cards from their Card Pool
  • Set up their Equipment

Once in a dungeon, the group will traverse zones of the dungeon. Some of these will feature Adventuring opportunities, and others will be Combat encounters.

As players work through these encounters, they will resolve objectives and earn gold by fighting enemies. At the end of each zone, the party will decide whether to continue or turn back, depending on how many of their cards are running out.

If the party leaves a dungeon, it will probably change and be repopulated with new dangers if they want to return to push deeper into the dungeon another time.

Once the players have cleared the dungeons in an area, they will move on to another settlement, where more adventures await.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text:
To keep combat engaging, CardPG has a number of Keywords:

Instant effects happen the moment the card is played and then they are done — there is nothing to track and nothing to remove.

Conditions are negative effects that linger on a target over multiple rounds. Most wear off on their own, but a Cure effect can remove them early.

Buffs are positive effects that strengthen a character for as long as they last. A Stun effect can strip them away.

If an ability has a Keyword, that keyword is activated on any hit, even if an attack does 0 damage.

docs.title: Instant Effects
docs.text: Keyword What it does Heal X Restore X health to yourself or a chosen ally. Restore X Pull X cards from your discard pile back into your hand. You choose which ones. Recover X Randomly retrieve X cards from your exile and return them to your discard pile. Cure X Remove X conditions from yourself or an ally. Cannot remove buffs. Pierce X This attack bypasses X points of the target's Bolster and armor — spent against Bolster first, then any leftover reduces armor. Bypass This attack ignores Shield entirely. Range X This attack can reach targets up to X spaces away. Push X Shove the target X spaces directly away from you. Pull X Drag the target X spaces directly toward you. Stun The target immediately loses one buff or one ongoing card effect. The defender chooses which. Coerce X Force one enemy to move up to X spaces toward another combatant and attack them for X damage. Muddle X Enemy ability. X of your cards are randomly exiled from your hand. Exhaust X Enemy ability. You must discard X cards from your hand of your choice. Ongoing This card enters your ongoing effect zone Expend This card is exiled immediately if used for its card effect.

docs.title: Conditions
docs.text:
Negative effects. Wear off over time, or remove them early with Cure.

Keyword What it does Burn Take 2 damage at the end of this round, then this condition clears. Bleed X Take 1 damage at the end of each round for X rounds. Freeze Move 1 fewer space on every movement action. Clears at end of round Curse X Take 1 additional damage from every attack you receive, for X rounds. Weaken X Deal 1 less damage with every attack you make, for X rounds. Blind You may only target enemies within 2 spaces, until end of round. Shock X Take 1 damage each time you make an attack, for up to X attacks. Poison X You cannot heal, restore, or recover cards for X rounds. Ignore positive Luck Die outcomes. If you rest while poisoned, you only restore half of your cards. Slow Your initiative drops to last place next round.

docs.title: Buffs
docs.text:
Positive effects. Fade over time, or stripped away by a Stun effect.

Keyword What it does Bolster X For the next X attacks against you, reduce damage by 1. Shield X The next X attacks against you are completely negated. Max 3 stacks. Retaliate X The next X times you are attacked, the attacker automatically takes 1 damage. Hide Enemies cannot target you. Lasts until you attack or target an enemy. Haste Gain +1 space on every movement action. Bless Your bad luck die results (1 and 2) have no effect on you. You are also immune to Curse, Poison and Weaken. Boost Your attacks roll at +2.

docs.title: Passive Abilities
docs.text: Every class and every weapon grants a passive ability that is always active — no card needs to be played to trigger it. These can significantly shape how you approach fights.

docs.title: Classes
docs.text:
• Barbarian: When you take damage, your next attack this round deals 1 additional damage.
  • Bard: Whenever you target another ally with an effect, they Cure 1 condition.
  • Cleric: Whenever you target an ally with a buff, heal them 1.
  • Druid: Your summons have +1 armor.
  • Knight: Permanent +1 armor.
  • Mage: When you play an Append card standalone (not appended to another attack), deal 1 additional damage.
  • Mercenary: When you attack a target that has a condition applied to them, deal 1 additional damage.
  • Monk: If you hit the same enemy twice in a turn, inflict Weaken 1.
  • Psychic: When you kill an enemy, regain 2 health.
  • Ranger: Gain 1 additional range on ranged attacks.
  • Rogue: The first time each round you attack from hidden, Restore 1 card from your discard.
  • Soldier: If an adjacent enemy moves away, inflict 1 damage.
  • Sorcerer: You may make basic attacks at Range 5.
  • Summoner: You can grant your basic moves and attacks to your summoned creatures.
  • Swashbuckler: Gain Boost when you make an attack with no allies within 3 spaces.
  • Witch: Enemies with 2 or more stacks of Curse deal 1 less damage against you.

docs.title: Weapons
docs.text:
• Bow: The first time each round an enemy enters an adjacent space, you may move 1.
  • Brawling: Your second and subsequent attacks each turn deal 1 additional damage.
  • Crossbow: If a single-target attack does 3+ damage, it also hits the enemy directly behind the target.
  • Cudgel: When you deal 3 or more damage in a single attack, apply Stun.
  • Daggers: Your attacks that deal Bleed, Weaken, or Poison deal one additional stack.
  • Greataxe: If you deal 4 damage to an enemy in a single hit, inflict Weaken 1.
  • Greatsword: When you attack, deal one extra damage if you've moved this turn.
  • Holy Symbol: At the start of each round of combat, gain Bless.
  • Mageblade: The first time each round you apply an elemental condition (Burn, Freeze, or Shock) with an attack, restore 1 card from your discard.
  • Rapier: When an enemy's melee attack deals no damage, immediately attack 1 against them.
  • Staff: Allies are immune to your AoE attacks.
  • Sword & Shield: The first time you attack each round, gain Bolster 1.
  • Tome: Whenever you apply Curse to an enemy, they also take 1 damage.
  • Wand: Your first attack each round gains Bypass.
  • Warhammer: Your melee attacks add Pierce 1.
  • Whipblade: When you Retaliate, you may move one space.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
There are 16 skills, divided into four categories:

Physical Skills

  • Athletic - used to perform feats of strength or physical labor.
  • Acrobatic - used to balance, leap, dodge, or climb
  • Sneaky - used to hide, move quietly, or look unassuming
  • Resilient - used to shrug off damage or push through pain

Social Skills

  • Personable - used to make a good first impression and appear harmless
  • Charming - used to manipulate, deceive, and bargain
  • Persuasive - used to reason and convince
  • Intimidating - used to threaten and control

Mental Skills

  • Clever - able to solve puzzles, think and respond quickly
  • Wise - able to see the best or most practical course of action
  • Learned - able to recall useful information
  • Resourceful - able to think laterally, attempt new solutions

Knowledge Skills

  • Arcana - knowledge of magic, spells, ancient languages
  • Nature - knowledge of nature, animals, plants and biomes
  • Martial - knowledge of military systems, weapons, defenses, strategy
  • Underworld - knowledge of illicit and illegal groups, connections

You start with 1 in each of your skills. You also gain skills based on your choices in character creation: Ancestry Skills
